title: Google I/O 2025 – 21tfI9O9RrA
text:
[MUSIC PLAYING] KUSHAGRA PATEL: Greetings, I/O.
I am Kushagra Patel, a product manager for Google Pay. Thank you for joining us today. We have been hard
at work to bring a ton of exciting new features
across Google Pay API and Google Wallet developer platforms to
enable even better journeys for your users. So let's get started
with Google Pay. Our mission at Google
Pay is to provide a simple, safe, and
a seamless payments experience for everyone,
everywhere, and help your business grow by
driving incremental sales. Today, we will look at
how we are continuing to make the most seamless and
accessible experience for users; the new use cases we are
enabling to meet your growing business needs; our commitment
to ensure Google Pay is the most secure way to pay; and updates
to our developer experience to bring the most delight to
you, our developer community. We are focused on delivering
a seamless one click checkout experience and making it
accessible across all surfaces for your users. We are very pleased to announce
that we have redesigned and re-engineered our
UX from the ground-up to ensure it drives even higher
conversion for your business. The newly designed
pay sheet now shows card-identifying information,
such as rich card art and name, to help users quickly
identify and select the card they want to pay with. Our UX is now optimized
to support dark mode, making it a more delightful
experience for your users while checking out. Next, we are thrilled to
announce the support for Google Pay on Android WebViews. More users are making purchases
using in-app browsers, and with Chrome
Version 136, your users will be able to
purchase seamlessly using Google Pay through an
Android-native experience, and also be able to use device
tokens from their Google Wallet, providing you with a
high-quality token. All you need is a single
change to your app manifest. Simply enable
Payment_Request and see the rest of it come to action. We introduced the Pay button
for Android last year. And you saw an
increase in sales when
speaker: unknown

title: Google I/O 2025 – 21tfI9O9RrA
text:
All you need is a single
change to your app manifest. Simply enable
Payment_Request and see the rest of it come to action. We introduced the Pay button
for Android last year. And you saw an
increase in sales when users saw their card-identifying
information on the button. We have not only
revamped the experience, but have added more
customization flexibility and are providing you
with more control. Now you can show
and hide the border and have added support
for more button copies to match your checkout screens. We also heard your
request to support presenting card-identifying
information when your UX does
not support a button. So in the coming months, we are
introducing Payment Metadata API that you can use to enable
similar functionality as the dynamic button. We are pleased to
announce upcoming support for merchant-initiated
transactions in the Google Pay Online API designed
to streamline payments for subscriptions, automatic
reloads, and deferred charges. Your customers will benefit
from enhanced pay sheet details, fostering trust and leading to a
more seamless checkout process, ultimately driving sales
growth for your business. With this feature, you
will be able to receive payment tokens not bound to
a customer specific device to ensure payment continuity
and prevent interruptions, even when a customer switches
their device in the future or modifies their Wallet. Finally, you will be able to
receive live cycle notifications for Google Pay payment tokens
for underlying credential changes, giving you time
to proactively reach out to your customers to update
their payment methods before the next billing cycle
improving recurring transaction continuity. We heard your request
to provide you finer control over what payment
cards you want to accept. So we are constantly
adding new features. You can now accept
or block cards based on the card
issuer country. We are enhancing our an API
to allow you to disburse funds to your users. This enhancement will allow
you to send money directly to your recipients cards.
speaker: unknown

title: Google I/O 2025 – 21tfI9O9RrA
text:
You can now accept
or block cards based on the card
issuer country. We are enhancing our an API
to allow you to disburse funds to your users. This enhancement will allow
you to send money directly to your recipients cards. Security and fraud prevention is
at the core of everything we do. Our latest fraud
detection models have shown a significant
reduction in payment fraud, driving lower cost
for your business. You can now report
chargeback data to Google Pay on
a periodic basis through a simple file upload
through our business console. We have the pipeline set
up that automatically process your uploaded
data and leverages it for tuning in our model further
help improve model precision, And when suspicious
activity is detected, Google can present
ID and [INAUDIBLE] challenge that the user must
pass to complete the purchase. This is available to
you today without you writing a single line of code. To empower you with greater
control over risk management, we plan to expand our
response parameters in the next few months. Please remember, Google
Pay validation and fraud checks are supplementary
and should not replace your established
risk management procedures. We also keep
developers top of mind, ensuring a delightful
developer experience. Testing your payment
flows with Google Pay is now easier with the latest
updates to the test card suite. See test cards for
your PSP of choice directly in the
payment sheet when using the API in the
test environment. Use these cards to run manual
or automated end-to-end tests and strengthen the quality
of your payment stories. Updates include regular
and tokenized test cards, debit cards,
and test cards now appear directly in
your Google Pay sheet when using the test
pay environment. We have heard from you
that error handling is a critical part of your
application and checkout flows. Today, we are
introducing improvements to our APIs that make it easier
to troubleshoot your Google Pay Integrations on
Android, giving you timely and detailed information
about the build and runtime
speaker: unknown

title: Google I/O 2025 – 21tfI9O9RrA
text:
is a critical part of your
application and checkout flows. Today, we are
introducing improvements to our APIs that make it easier
to troubleshoot your Google Pay Integrations on
Android, giving you timely and detailed information
about the build and runtime errors. Now you can fix your
integrations at dev time more quickly with fine-grained
error logs and react to unexpected scenarios at
runtime with detailed exceptions and error codes. We recently published resources
designed for web developers just starting out with
the Google Pay API. These resources work
together, providing for a delightful
onboarding experience. The Codelabs provides
step-by-step walkthroughs of a working implementation. The IDX templates provide
one-click development environments with
ready-to-run implementations. And third, the
Learning Pathway is a guided tour of the API from
concept to implementation. This year, we plan to
launch similar resources for native Android,
Flutter, React.js, React Native, and Angular. You will find links to
these resources and more in the video's description. We know that your
business relies on continuous
availability of our APIs. Last year, our services
had an uptime of 99.99%, and we are committed to
having high availability. To pair with this, we want
to announce the availability of our self-serve Google
Pay API Status Dashboard where you can find real-time
information on the health of Google Pay API products. The dashboard monitors
key Google Pay APIs, including the CreateButton,
the IsReadyToPay API, and the LoadPaymentData API. You can stay abreast with
incidents impacting availability of any of these key services and
updates through this dashboard. Beyond the website, you can
access the Status Dashboard also via the RSS feed and
the JSON history file, allowing for programmatic
consumption of the data. Remember, the Google
Pay API Status Dashboard is designed to be available
even if the APIs themselves are experiencing an issue. EDSON YANAGA: Hello IO, I'm
Edson Yanaga, a developer
speaker: unknown

title: Google I/O 2025 – 21tfI9O9RrA
text:
allowing for programmatic
consumption of the data. Remember, the Google
Pay API Status Dashboard is designed to be available
even if the APIs themselves are experiencing an issue. EDSON YANAGA: Hello IO, I'm
Edson Yanaga, a developer relations engineer
for Google Wallet, and I'm super excited to talk
about what we've been doing. Last year, we announced the
Google Wallet global expansion. Since then, Google Wallet
is available in more than 90 countries and territories,
with dozens more coming soon. We've worked hard to make
Google Wallet more robust, more flexible, and ultimately
more feature-rich for you. This means providing you
with enhanced features and documentations to build
innovative engagement strategies that not only simplify
daily transactions, but also foster a deeper
connection between Google Wallet and your users. Digital IDs in Google Wallet
are available in US markets today and are built
with trust, ease of use, and interoperability
as our top priorities. This is why we're working to
bring more digital IDs to Google Wallet to support our
users across the globe. Today, we are live
in six US states, with more states coming soon. If you have a US passport,
and soon, a British passport, you can also create a
digital ID called an ID Pass with your passport and
store it in Google Wallet. British users will be able to
use their ID Pass for proving that they meet the eligibility
criteria for select railcards purchased through UK-based
Rail Delivery Group's railcard-retaining
platform, railcard.co.uk. To enable your users to
leverage these digital IDs, Google worked together with
ecosystem partners in the W3C and introduced our
Digital Credentials API. This API can be used
by a digital wallet and provides a unified
and secure framework for apps and websites to
request verifiable proof of age or identity directly from any
digital wallet in the user's device. The API was designed
to be interoperable with multi-wallet
support across devices and operating systems
with a single integration.
speaker: unknown

title: Google I/O 2025 – 21tfI9O9RrA
text:
for apps and websites to
request verifiable proof of age or identity directly from any
digital wallet in the user's device. The API was designed
to be interoperable with multi-wallet
support across devices and operating systems
with a single integration. This system prioritizes
user privacy through encrypted credentials,
standardized data formats for interoperability,
and selective disclosure where only necessary attributes
such as age above 18 are shared. The Digital
Credentials API will be leveraged by developers for
agent identity verification across a variety of use cases. Notably, Google has
developed our own innovative zero-knowledge proof technology
that allows for age verification without any possibility
to link back to a user's personal identity. We will be open sourcing
the ZKP libraries to help make the
internet a safer place for kids and families. The API is available
to your Android apps through the Credential
Manager Jetpack Library. It's also available on
Chrome on mobile web, and on desktop
via Origin Trials. And it will soon
work cross-device, providing a seamless
experience between Chrome on desktop and your
Android device. To learn more about how to
get started with the Digital Credentials API, please
watch the Chrome session on authentication and identity. Now, I'll let Kevin talk
about Wallet Passes. KEVIN MILLER: Thanks, Edson. I'm Kevin Miller,
a senior product manager for Google
Wallet, and we have a lot of things to talk about. First thing, we're very
excited to introduce Google Wallet support for kids. Parents and guardians
in select countries can now give their
kids the freedom to tap and pay in stores,
plus keep supported passes like event tickets,
library cards, and gift cards all
in one place right on their kids' Android devices. Safety is key, so parents have
controls with Family Link. They will get notified by
email about every transaction and can easily track recent
purchases, remove payment cards, and turn off Passes
access all in Family Link.
speaker: unknown

title: Google I/O 2025 – 21tfI9O9RrA
text:
Safety is key, so parents have
controls with Family Link. They will get notified by
email about every transaction and can easily track recent
purchases, remove payment cards, and turn off Passes
access all in Family Link. Best of all, there are no
changes in the Google Wallet API to support this enhancement. Last year, we expanded
mobile push notifications for the Google Wallet API,
empowering you to deliver timely updates to your users. This year, we're taking
it a step further with the introduction of
Field Update Notifications. Imagine being able to trigger
a push notification, not just on a general update,
but specifically when a particular field
within a pass changes. For example, when a user's point
balance crosses a threshold or when their tier
status is upgraded, a push notification
can be triggered, which will notify them that the
data on their pass has changed. This granularity allows
for a much more engaging and user-centric experience,
driving higher engagement and utility on the pass. Speaking of notifications,
we're really excited to announce that we
will support nearby Passes notifications for the
Google Wallet API. If enabled by the
user, this feature is designed to provide timely
and relevant information by alerting users about
pertinent passes such as loyalty cards, offers, boarding
passes, or event tickets when they approach a
designated point of interest. The Google Wallet app sends
a contextual notification when the user is near
a specific location. This notification serves
as a direct gateway, allowing users to seamlessly
access the associated pass with a single tap. This direct access promotes
a more fluid and intuitive interaction with
the stored passes, encouraging users to leverage
the wallets capabilities more effectively. To ensure user control
and flexibility, we've introduced two new
toggles to help users control their notifications experience. The first is on the
Pass Detail Screen that allows users to turn
on or off notifications from that specific pass.
speaker: unknown

title: Google I/O 2025 – 21tfI9O9RrA
text:
To ensure user control
and flexibility, we've introduced two new
toggles to help users control their notifications experience. The first is on the
Pass Detail Screen that allows users to turn
on or off notifications from that specific pass. This applies to
all notifications related to your pass, including
field updates and nearby passes notifications. The second is through the nearby
Passes Notifications Channel that allows users to
control whether they receive nearby passes notifications. This empowers users to tailor
their notification settings based on their specific
needs and preferences. Engaging users goes
beyond just notifications, and we recognize that you
have more value you want to make your users aware of. To address this, we're
introducing Value Added Opportunities, which
is a significant step towards transforming passes
into a more dynamic engagement surface for you and your users. This feature empowers you to
integrate personalized modules directly into your passes,
showcasing relevant deals, promotions, and
additional services. By highlighting these
value-added benefits, such as exclusive offers
or upgrade options, you can guide users back
to your app or website, creating a dynamic gateway
for ongoing user interaction. Users have saved
millions of passes by manually adding their
loyalty cards to Google Wallet. These passes are unlinked
from their merchant accounts, and as a developer, you
can't update or engage users on these passes. We've now introduced a
pass upgrade experience that prompts users to sign
into their merchant account and save a linked
version of the pass. All you need to do is integrate
with the Wallet API's user Loyalty Program
Enrollment feature and ensure that users can
save the linked pass once they successfully sign in. We'll be expanding on
this feature in the future to enable users who have added
a pass using our Everything Else feature to link to the
merchant account as well. At last year's I/O, we announced
Auto Linked Passes, allowing
speaker: unknown

title: Google I/O 2025 – 21tfI9O9RrA
text:
We'll be expanding on
this feature in the future to enable users who have added
a pass using our Everything Else feature to link to the
merchant account as well. At last year's I/O, we announced
Auto Linked Passes, allowing you to add an additional
related pass automatically to your user's Google Wallet,
provided they already have an existing pass issued by you. Now, we're happy to
announce an expansion of this feature for airlines. Developers from
airlines that integrate loyalty cards for their
frequent flyer programs with the Google Wallet
API can automatically push boarding passes
to their users' wallets once they check in for a flight. That's another way
that Google Wallet is making your user's
travel journey even better. Google Wallet users already
benefit from streamlined travel on open-loop EMV transit systems
using tokenized payment methods for seamless fare transactions. Our Google Transit
Insight solution further enhances this
experience by providing riders with detailed journey and
fare construction details. The tokenized
open-loop payment card acts as a bridge between user
payments and transit systems, facilitating this communication. Building on this
foundation, we're excited to announce the upcoming
expansion of Google Transit Insights to support specific
pass types sales such as season passes directly
linked to the user's tokenized open-loop
payment card. With this new
capability, developers will soon be able to leverage
the Google transit API to implement seamless pass
purchasing and management, eliminating the need for
separate transit cards. Users of Google Wallet will
now see real-time transit pass updates, such as on-time
or delayed train status, directly on their passes. This enhanced
experience is powered by the Google Wallet
API's new Live Status Support and a seamless
integration with Google Maps. Train operators can
enable this feature by ensuring their tickets
contain the required fields and providing a
real time GTFS feed, making it accessible to
major operators worldwide.
speaker: unknown

title: Google I/O 2025 – 21tfI9O9RrA
text:
Support and a seamless
integration with Google Maps. Train operators can
enable this feature by ensuring their tickets
contain the required fields and providing a
real time GTFS feed, making it accessible to
major operators worldwide. For some use cases,
it's important for you to create a pass that includes a
profile photo of the user you're distributing the pass to. To achieve this, we're enabling
secure private images on passes. With this feature,
you're able to define passes that include
images that are only accessible to the
holder of the pass. For example, this allows you to
create digital business cards, membership passes,
or event tickets with the user
profile image on it. Please note that
this feature can't be used for official
identity verification. Join the Developer
Advisory Council and shape the future of Google
Pay and Google Wallet APIs. Members may provide feedback
to influence API design, engage with the Google Pay
and Google Wallet teams, and participate in
exclusive events. Find a link to a
membership interest form in the video's description. What a fantastic year it's
been for Google Pay and Google Wallet. We've seen incredible progress
across both platforms delivering real value to
developers and users, and we're just getting started. We truly hope that
no matter what you're building, from the
core of payments to the latest in digital
IDs, engaging retail sites to rewarding loyalty
programs, you've discovered something
inspiring today. We can't wait to see the amazing
things you'll build and innovate with the Google Pay
and Google Wallet APIs. Have a wonderful. Google I/O, everyone. Thank you so much
for watching us. [MUSIC PLAYING]
speaker: unknown
